Title: MAP KINASE PHOSPHATASE1 promotes osmotolerance by suppressing PHYTOALEXIN DEFICIENT4-independent immunity

Plant_Genes.Notes_or_Other_Important_Info: The aod13 mutant allele is equivalent to a loss-of-function mkp1 allele in the Bu-5 background, which lacks functional ACQOS; thus, MKP1-dependent control of immunity and osmotolerance operates independently of ACQOS-mediated EDS1/PAD4-dependent autoimmunity characterized previously in Col-0.
Plant_Genes.Summary_key_Findings_of_Core_Gene:
1. Forward genetic screening in the Bu-5 accession identified the acquired osmotolerance-defective mutant aod13, which is hypersensitive to osmotic and osmo-shock stress but shows normal growth and ABA sensitivity under non-stress conditions.
2. High-resolution mapping, whole-genome resequencing, and complementation demonstrated that AOD13 encodes MKP1 and that aod13 carries a 1-bp deletion causing a frameshift and truncation of the C-terminal MAPK-binding domain.
3. Loss of MKP1 in aod13 results in elevated MPK3/6 activation under osmotic stress and reduced induction of several DREB/CBF regulon target genes, indicating that MKP1 negatively regulates MPK3/6 and influences osmotic-stress transcriptional responses.
4. aod13 mutants are more tolerant to salt stress than Bu-5 WT and aod13_MKP1, with lower shoot Na+ accumulation, establishing MKP1 as a negative regulator of salt tolerance and a positive regulator of osmotic-stress tolerance.
5. aod13 exhibits enhanced immune responses, including elevated PR1, PR5, PR1, PAD3, FRK1, WRKY29, and EXO70H4 expression under osmotic stress; in pad4 aod13, these defense responses remain strongly induced and osmotolerance is not restored, showing that MKP1 promotes osmotolerance by repressing PAD4-independent immunity activated downstream of MPK3/6.
Plant_Genes.Omics_Data: Whole-genome resequencing of aod13 and Bu-5 using Illumina HiSeq 2500 (1 × 100 bp single reads); reads deposited in DDBJ Read Archive under accession DRA012094.
Plant_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: MKP1, encoded by AOD13, promotes acquired osmotolerance to osmotic stress in an ABA-independent manner and suppresses PAD4-independent immune activation by negatively regulating MPK3/6 activity; loss of MKP1 (aod13/mkp1) causes hypersensitivity to osmotic and osmo-shock stress but increases tolerance to salt stress by reducing Na+ accumulation in shoots.
Plant_Genes.Regulatory_Mechanism: Acts as a dual-specificity phosphatase that negatively regulates MPK3/6 activation; loss of MKP1 leads to elevated MPK3/6 activity under osmotic stress, reduced induction of DREB/CBF regulon genes, and enhanced PAD4-independent activation of immune marker genes such as PR1, PAD3, FRK1, WRKY29, and EXO70H4.
Plant_Genes.Regulatory_Pathway: Functions in MAPK (MPK3/6) signaling to integrate abiotic stress responses (DREB/CBF regulon-mediated osmotic stress tolerance) with immune signaling, suppressing PAD4-independent defense pathways under osmotic stress.
Plant_Genes.Upstream_or_Downstream_Regulation: MKP1 --(inhibition via dephosphorylation)--> MPK3/6 activation; loss of MKP1 in aod13 leads to increased MPK3/6 activity under osmotic stress and strong induction of PR1, PAD3, FRK1, WRKY29, and EXO70H4, indicating that MKP1 acts upstream as a negative regulator of PAD4-independent immune-gene activation and influences DREB/CBF regulon expression.
Plant_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Dual-specificity MAP kinase phosphatase with a C-terminal MAPK-binding domain that is truncated by the frameshift mutation in aod13.
Plant_Genes.Key_Variant_Site: aod13 carries a 1-bp deletion in the MKP1 coding region that causes a frameshift and truncation of the C-terminal MAPK-binding domain (genic coding variant, affects_function).
Plant_Genes.Key_Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Osmotic stress imposed by 750 mM sorbitol following 7 d acclimation at 100 mM NaCl; osmo-shock stress imposed by direct transfer to 600 mM sorbitol; salt stress imposed by 100–225 mM NaCl for Na+ accumulation and tolerance assays; flg22 peptide treatment used as a positive control for MPK3/6 activation.
Plant_Genes.Research_Topic: Acquired osmotolerance, salt tolerance, and immune regulation under osmotic stress in Arabidopsis.
Plant_Genes.Trait_Category: Abiotic stress tolerance (acquired osmotolerance to osmotic stress, osmo-shock tolerance, salt tolerance) and biotic stress / immune response (autoimmunity and defense-gene activation).
Plant_Genes.Other_Phenotypic_Effects: Under non-stress growth on soil, aod13 plants show similar morphology and fresh weight to Bu-5 WT, and ABA sensitivity is not significantly altered compared with WT.
Plant_Genes.Experimental_Materials: Arabidopsis thaliana accession Bu-5 (nonfunctional ACQOS), ion-beam–mutagenized M2 and derived aod13 mutant (mkp1 loss-of-function in Bu-5 background), F1 and F2 progeny from aod13 × Pog-0 cross, transgenic aod13_MKP1 lines carrying genomic MKP1 under its native promoter, pad4 aod13 double mutant generated by CRISPR/Cas9, and comparison lines including Bu-5 WT and Col-0-based lines used for some assays.
Plant_Genes.Variety: Bu-5 accession (nonfunctional ACQOS allele) and derived mutant aod13
Plant_Genes.Experimental_Methods: Acquired osmotolerance assays involving pre-treatment of seedlings with 100 mM NaCl followed by transfer to 750 mM sorbitol, osmo-shock assays using 600 mM sorbitol, and salt-tolerance assays using 225–400 mM NaCl; chlorophyll quantification from stressed seedlings; high-resolution mapping with SSLP markers in F2 plants; whole-genome resequencing and variant calling against TAIR10; Agrobacterium-mediated transformation with genomic MKP1 constructs and selection of T3 homozygous lines; MAPK activity assays using sorbitol or flg22 treatment followed by anti-pTEpY immunoblot; Na+ content measurement in shoots by ion chromatography; RT-qPCR of DREB/CBF regulon, PR1, PR5, PAD3, FRK1, WRKY29, and EXO70H4; and CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing of PAD4 followed by T7E1 assay and Sanger sequencing.
Plant_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Forward mutant isolation (aod13), high-resolution mapping and whole-genome resequencing, genomic complementation with native promoter:MKP1 (aod13_MKP1), phenotyping in acquired osmotolerance, osmo-shock, and salt-stress assays with chlorophyll and survival measurements, MAPK activity immunoblot to assess MPK3/6 activation, Na+ content measurements, and genetic interaction with CRISPR-generated pad4 aod13 double mutant combined with RT-qPCR of stress- and defense-related marker genes.
Plant_Genes.Genetic_Population: 30,000 ion-beam–mutagenized M2 seedlings of Bu-5 screened for acquired osmotolerance-defective mutants; F2 mapping population derived from a cross between aod13 (Bu-5 background) and the osmotolerant accession Pog-0 used for high-resolution mapping of the causal locus.
Plant_Genes.Gene_Discovery_or_Cloning_Method: Forward genetic screen of 30,000 M2 seedlings from ion-beam–mutagenized Bu-5 for acquired osmotolerance-defective mutants (aod). High-resolution mapping of aod13 using an F2 population from a cross with Pog-0 localized the causal locus to a 222-kbp interval on chromosome 3. Whole-genome resequencing of aod13 and Bu-5 identified nonsynonymous mutations in six genes in this interval, and complementation tests with genomic fragments showed that only MKP1 restored acquired osmotolerance; resequencing revealed a 1-bp deletion in the MKP1 coding region in aod13.
Plant_Genes.Breeding_Application_Value: MKP1 is a positive regulator of acquired osmotolerance but a negative regulator of salt tolerance in Arabidopsis. Loss-of-function (aod13/mkp1) enhances salt tolerance by reducing shoot Na+ accumulation but causes strong hypersensitivity to osmotic stress accompanied by autoimmune activation, whereas complementation with genomic MKP1 restores WT-like osmotolerance and salt sensitivity. MKP1 thus represents a potential breeding or engineering target to balance osmotic-stress tolerance, salinity tolerance, and immune activation, but manipulation would require careful management of the trade-off between improved salt tolerance/stronger immunity and reduced osmotolerance.

Plant_Genes.Notes_or_Other_Important_Info: pad4 aod13 plants used in this study are T3-generation null segregants that have lost the CRISPR/Cas9 T-DNA but retain a homozygous PAD4 frameshift mutation; comparison with Bu-5 WT and aod13 underscores that PAD4 loss does not mimic previously reported pad4-mediated acquired osmotolerance in Col-0 and that MKP1-dependent control of osmotolerance operates largely through PAD4-independent immune pathways in the Bu-5 background.
Plant_Genes.Summary_key_Findings_of_Core_Gene:
1. PAD4 was specifically knocked out in the aod13 (mkp1) background using a CRISPR/Cas9 system, generating pad4 aod13 plants carrying a 1-bp insertion in the second exon that causes a frameshift and premature stop codon.
2. In contrast to previous findings in Col-0, where pad4 single mutants confer acquired osmotolerance, pad4 aod13 plants in the Bu-5 background do not acquire osmotolerance and remain osmosensitive like aod13.
3. Under osmotic stress, PR1 and PAD3, as well as FRK1, WRKY29, and EXO70H4, are strongly induced in pad4 aod13, although PR1 expression is somewhat lower than in aod13, demonstrating that defense activation in the MKP1-deficient background is largely PAD4-independent.
4. These results show that MKP1 promotes osmotolerance by suppressing PAD4-independent immunity and that PAD4 is not a major determinant of osmotic-stress sensitivity in the aod13 background lacking functional ACQOS.
Plant_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: In the Bu-5 background carrying mkp1 (aod13), loss of PAD4 (pad4 aod13) does not restore acquired osmotolerance and plants remain hypersensitive to osmotic stress; immune marker genes PR1 and PAD3, as well as FRK1, WRKY29, and EXO70H4, are still strongly induced under osmotic stress, though PR1 induction is somewhat reduced compared with aod13. Thus, the enhanced defense activation and osmosensitive phenotype of aod13 are largely PAD4-independent.
Plant_Genes.Regulatory_Mechanism: CRISPR/Cas9-induced frameshift in PAD4 disrupts its coding sequence; however, under osmotic stress in the mkp1 (aod13) background, PAD4 is not required for the strong activation of PR1, PAD3, FRK1, WRKY29, and EXO70H4 or for the osmosensitive phenotype, indicating that the dominant immune activation pathway in aod13 is PAD4-independent.
Plant_Genes.Regulatory_Pathway: Previously known EDS1–PAD4-dependent autoimmunity is contrasted with a PAD4-independent immune pathway; in this study, osmotic stress in mkp1 (aod13) plants activates defense responses predominantly through a PAD4-independent pathway, even when PAD4 function is eliminated.
Plant_Genes.Key_Variant_Site: pad4 aod13 carries a 1-bp insertion in the second exon of PAD4 that causes a frameshift and premature termination codon (genic coding variant, affects_function).
Plant_Genes.Key_Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Osmotic stress imposed by 100 mM NaCl pre-treatment for 7 d followed by 750 mM sorbitol exposure in the acquired osmotolerance assay, under which defense gene induction and osmotolerance phenotypes of Bu-5 WT, aod13, and pad4 aod13 were compared.
Plant_Genes.Research_Topic: Role of PAD4-dependent versus PAD4-independent immunity in the regulation of acquired osmotolerance under osmotic stress in Arabidopsis.
Plant_Genes.Trait_Category: Abiotic stress tolerance (acquired osmotolerance under osmotic stress) and biotic stress / immune response (PAD4-dependent versus PAD4-independent immunity).
Plant_Genes.Other_Phenotypic_Effects: Compared with aod13, pad4 aod13 shows slightly reduced PR1 induction under osmotic stress but retains strong induction of PAD3, FRK1, WRKY29, and EXO70H4, and remains as osmosensitive as aod13, indicating that PAD4 modulates but is not essential for the hyperactivation of defense genes in the MKP1-deficient background.
Plant_Genes.Experimental_Materials: Arabidopsis thaliana Bu-5 WT (ACQOS-nonfunctional), aod13 (mkp1 loss-of-function), and CRISPR/Cas9-derived pad4 aod13 double mutant lines (T3 generation null segregants lacking T-DNA and carrying a homozygous PAD4 frameshift).
Plant_Genes.Variety: Bu-5 accession background carrying aod13 (mkp1) and CRISPR-derived pad4 aod13 double mutant
Plant_Genes.Experimental_Methods: Construction of pGK-YC9 all-in-one CRISPR-Cas9 vector targeting PAD4, transformation and selection of edited plants, T7 endonuclease I assay and PCR-based genotyping, Sanger sequencing to confirm PAD4 frameshift mutation, acquired osmotolerance assay (100 mM NaCl pre-treatment followed by 750 mM sorbitol), chlorophyll content measurements, and RT-qPCR of PR1, PAD3, FRK1, WRKY29, and EXO70H4 in Bu-5 WT, aod13, and pad4 aod13 under normal and osmotic-stress conditions.
Plant_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR/Cas9-mediated knockout of PAD4 in the aod13 background to generate pad4 aod13 lines, confirmation of a 1-bp insertion causing frameshift and premature stop codon by sequencing, phenotyping pad4 aod13 in the acquired osmotolerance assay alongside Bu-5 WT and aod13, and RT-qPCR analysis of PR1, PAD3, FRK1, WRKY29, and EXO70H4 expression under normal and osmotic-stress conditions.
Plant_Genes.Gene_Discovery_or_Cloning_Method: PAD4 was not mapped or cloned de novo in this study; instead, an all-in-one CRISPR-Cas9 vector (pGK-YC9) was used to introduce a targeted mutation into PAD4 in the aod13 background. A complementary oligonucleotide pair containing the PAD4 target sequence was cloned into the vector, and T1 seedlings were screened by T7 endonuclease I assay and PCR; null segregants in the T2 generation carrying a homozygous PAD4 mutation were confirmed by Sanger sequencing and used as pad4 aod13 lines.
Plant_Genes.Breeding_Application_Value: In the Bu-5 background lacking functional ACQOS, CRISPR/Cas9-mediated knockout of PAD4 in the mkp1 mutant (pad4 aod13) does not restore acquired osmotolerance and PAD4-independent immune responses remain strongly activated under osmotic stress. These data indicate that targeting PAD4 alone is unlikely to improve osmotolerance when MKP1 function is compromised, highlighting MKP1 and downstream MAPK signaling rather than PAD4 as primary breeding or engineering targets for modulating osmotolerance in such genetic backgrounds.
